title: Major Decisions
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Making Big Decisions
summary: Scripture offers wisdom for decision-making that combines trust in God's sovereignty with the practical use of counsel, prayer, and discernment rather than waiting for miraculous signs.
body:
The Bible does not promise a burning bush for every fork in the road. But it does provide a framework for decision-making that begins with trust: in all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make your paths straight [verse:prov.3.5-6]. Acknowledging God does not mean waiting passively — it means bringing him into the process.

James addresses the believer who lacks wisdom: ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault [verse:jas.1.5]. The promise is not that the answer will arrive as an audible voice but that God is generous with the wisdom needed to navigate uncertainty.

Proverbs values community in decision-making. Plans fail for lack of counsel, but with many advisors they succeed [verse:prov.15.22]. God often speaks through the collective wisdom of trusted people who know you, your situation, and the Scriptures.

Paul models discernment through spiritual reasoning. He writes about his travel plans not as divine dictation but as careful judgment informed by circumstances, community needs, and the Spirit's leading [verse:acts.16.6-10]. Sometimes the Spirit redirects; sometimes the open door is the guidance.

The psalmist asks God to teach him the way he should go [verse:ps.143.8]. The posture is receptive but not passive. Moses reminds Israel that God has given them the capacity to choose: I have set before you life and death, blessings and curses. Now choose life [verse:deut.30.19]. God honors human agency even as he guides it.

The deepest comfort in decision-making is not certainty about the outcome but confidence in the Guide. Even when the path is unclear, the one who walks it with you is faithful.

verses.annotation: Trust the Lord and acknowledge him in every decision — he directs the path.
Ask God for wisdom — he gives generously and without reproach.
Plans succeed with many advisors — wise counsel as a means of guidance.
Paul's Macedonian call — the Spirit guiding through a combination of closed doors and vision.
Teach me the way I should go — a prayer of receptive dependence.
Choose life — God presents options and honors human agency.
A renewed mind able to test and approve God's good, pleasing, and perfect will.

scholars.quote: Longman reads Proverbs 3:5-6 not as a guarantee of a trouble-free path but as a promise that the person who trusts God will look back and see coherence — the straightening often becomes visible only in hindsight.
Davids emphasizes that James 1:5 promises wisdom, not information — God does not hand out blueprints but cultivates the discernment needed to navigate complexity faithfully.

Keener reads Acts 16:6-10 as Luke showing that divine guidance often operates through a mix of closed doors, circumstantial evidence, and community discernment rather than unmistakable supernatural signs.
